coredata.dc:title: Europium doped silicon nanoparticles for “turn-on” ratiometric fluorescent discrimination of leucine enantiomers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Yaning
Liu, Chen
Lu, Cancan
Liao, Xuan
Ma, Liwei
Ren, Cuiling
Chen, Hongli
coredata.dc:description: d-leucine has potential significance in preventing epilepsy seizures and diagnosing diabetes, so detecting d-leucine, especially in its racemate is crucial. In this paper, chiral fluorescent silicon nanoparticles Eu-LSiNPs were synthesized at room temperature and a dual emission system Eu-LSiNPs/TC based on the “antenna effect” between Eu3+ and tetracycline (TC) was developed. Eu-LSiNPs/TC showed two distinctive emission at 510 nm and 616 nm, its fluorescence at 510 nm can be significantly enhanced while the signal at 616 nm can be decreased by d-leucine, whereas l-leucine has less impact on the fluorescence intensity of Eu-LSiNPs/TC, indicating that it can be used as an effective “turn-on” ratiometric fluorescent probe for discriminating leucine enantiomers. The sensitivity of Eu-LSiNPs/TC towards d-leucine is 18.4-fold higher than that of l-leucine. Compared with single emission nanosensors, Eu-LSiNPs/TC showed better stability and repeatability. Lastly, its chiral discrimination mechanism towards Leu enantiomers was systematically investigated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral discrimination
Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Leucine enantiomers
Turn on
Europium doped silicon nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chiral recognition of amino acid enantiomers

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of Eu-LSiNPs; (B) CD spectra of Eu-LSiNPs and d-Glu; (C) FTIR spectrum; (D) XPS survey spectrum of Eu-LSiNPs.
The recognition ability of Eu-LSiNPs/TC towards common amino acid enantiomers under the same conditions (The concentration of amino acids are all 10 mM, except Asp is 4 mM, Glu is 5 mM).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Eu-LSiNPs/TC after adding different concentrations of d-Leu and (B) Linear relationship between (F − F0)510/F616 and the concentration of d-Leu; (C) Fluorescence spectra of Eu-LSiNPs/TC after adding different concentrations of l-Leu; (D) Linear relationship between (F − F0)510/F616 and the concentration of l-Leu.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of Eu-LSiNPs/TC after adding different ratios of l-Leu and d-Leu; (B) Linear relationship between (F − F0)510/F616 and the ratio of d-Leu.

(A) FT-IR spectra of Eu-LSiNPs/TC before and after the addition of d-Leu; (B) UV–Vis spectra of Eu-LSiNPs/TC before and after the addition of L/d-Leu; (C) Fluorescence decay curves of Eu-LSiNPs/TC before and after the addition of L/d-Leu; (D) Zeta potential diagrams of Eu-LSiNPs/TC before and after the addition of L/d-Leu.

(A) VB-XPS spectra of Eu-LSiNPs/TC and (B) after the addition of d-Leu; (C) UV–Vis spectra and Tauc plot diagrams of the blank and (D) after the addition of d-Leu; (E) Possible sensing mechanism of Eu-LSiNPs/TC to d-Leu.
Schematic diagram of the construction of Eu-LSiNPs/TC and its application in leucine enantiomer recognition.

head.ce:title: Europium doped silicon nanoparticles for “turn-on” ratiometric fluorescent discrimination of leucine enantiomers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: d-leucine has potential significance in preventing epilepsy seizures and diagnosing diabetes, so detecting d-leucine, especially in its racemate is crucial. In this paper, chiral fluorescent silicon nanoparticles Eu-LSiNPs were synthesized at room temperature and a dual emission system Eu-LSiNPs/TC based on the “antenna effect” between Eu3+ and tetracycline (TC) was developed. Eu-LSiNPs/TC showed two distinctive emission at 510 nm and 616 nm, its fluorescence at 510 nm can be significantly enhanced while the signal at 616 nm can be decreased by d-leucine, whereas l-leucine has less impact on the fluorescence intensity of Eu-LSiNPs/TC, indicating that it can be used as an effective “turn-on” ratiometric fluorescent probe for discriminating leucine enantiomers. The sensitivity of Eu-LSiNPs/TC towards d-leucine is 18.4-fold higher than that of l-leucine. Compared with single emission nanosensors, Eu-LSiNPs/TC showed better stability and repeatability. Lastly, its chiral discrimination mechanism towards Leu enantiomers was systematically investigated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets used and/or analysed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the common branched-chain amino acids in proteins, l-leucine helps promote muscle recovery after training and also promotes the healing of skin, bones, and damaged muscle tissue, regulating cardiac damage driven by cardiac cachexia. l-leucine can also stimulate insulin and assist in regulating human blood sugar levels [1,2]. However, there is relatively little research on d-leucine. Currently, it has been reported that d-leucine can effectively terminate the onset of epilepsy in patients [3]. In addition, studies on mice have found that d-leucine can protect oocytes from psychological stress by reducing oxidative stress [4]. Therefore, distinguishing the enantiomers of leucine is of great significance for subsequent research on the physiological functions of its single enantiomer.

Several methods for leucine enantiomer recognition have been reported, including electrochemical method [5], HPLC-MS [6], Raman spectroscopy method [7], colorimetric method [8], fluorescent method [9] and so on [10]. In these methods, fluorescence method has been widely studied because of its advantages of simple operation, high sensitivity, and high visual selectivity. For fluorescent detection, the design and synthesis of fluorescent probes are crucial. Currently, the reported fluorescent probes include organic small molecules [11–13], quantum dots [14], carbon dots [15], and fluorescent silicon nanoparticles [16]. Compared with other nanoparticles, fluorescent silicon nanoparticles are regarded as a promising nanomaterial due to their advantages such as high fluorescence quantum yield, good photobleaching resistance, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and great application prospects in the fields of sensing [17], chiral recognition [18], and bioimaging [19].

The previously reported fluorescent probes for recognizing amino acid enantiomers are generally based on fluorescence quenching. This “turn off” mode are typically affected by low fluorescence efficiency and self-quenching and are prone to generate false fluorescence signals. In contrast to it, the fluorescence “turn-on” mode, which based on the enhancement fluorescence signal triggered by the analytes, possesses higher selectivity and reliability [20–22]. The “antenna effect” of lanthanide elements refers to the phenomenon where lanthanide elements combine with some organic ligands, which can absorb ultraviolet light and transfer energy to lanthanide ions, thereby greatly enhancing their luminescence. [23] Due to antenna effect, dual emission fluorescent probes were constructed. Compared with single emission probes, which are sensitive to the instrument parameters, microenvironment, local concentration of probe, etc. [24–26], ratiometric fluorescence probes can greatly improve sensing sensitivity and accuracy because they can provide reliable self-calibration [27]. Therefore, it is of great significance to design and develop a “turn-on” ratio-enhanced fluorescent probe to discriminate leucine enantiomers.

For these reasons, this work prepared a new chiral fluorescent silicon nanoparticles Eu-LSiNPs by N-(2-aminoethyl)-3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (DAMO), thioglycolic acid (TGA), l-Glu and europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O) at room temperature. Tetracycline enhances the fluorescence of Eu3+ at 616 nm through the “antenna effect”, thereby constructing a ratiometric nanoprobe Eu-LSiNPs/TC for leucine enantiomer discrimination (Scheme 1 ).

N-(2-aminoethyl)-3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (DAMO) was purchased from Sigma-Reagents Co., Ltd. Europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O), tetracycline (TC), L/d-glutamic acid, L/d-leucine, L/d-histidine, L/d-cysteine, L/d-alanine and other amino acids were all purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

Fluorescence spectra were scanned using a RF-5301 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The particle size of Eu-LSiNPs was determined by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were determined with a Nicolet Nexus 670 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of Eu-LSiNPs was measured by an Axis Supra spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Valence band X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (VB-XPS) spectra. The fluorescence lifetime was measured using the time-correlated single photon counting system of the FL920P spectrometer. A J1500 comprehensive circular polarization spectroscopy tester (JASCO, Japan) was employed to measure the circular dichroism spectra.

0.0294 g of l-glutamic acid was dissolved in 2.5 mL of ultrapure water. Then, 3 mL of Eu(NO3)3·6H2O solution (2.0 mmol/L) was added, followed by the addition of 15 μL of mercaptoacetic acid. The mixture was subjected to ultrasonication for 5 min and stirring for 5 min to achieve complete dissolution. 0.5 mL of DAMO was added and the mixture was stirred for 48 h. The obtained product was dialyzed (0–500 Da) for 24 h to obtain the Eu-LSiNPs solution, which was stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chiral recognition of amino acid enantiomers
ce:section.ce:para: 100 μL of Eu-LSiNPs was dispersed in a Hepes buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.10) containing 100 μL of TC (1 mM) to obtain the Eu-LSiNPs/TC sensing system. Different concentrations of leucine enantiomer solutions were then added separately. The total volume of the solution was maintained at 3 mL, and fluorescence tests were carried out after reacting at room temperature for 10 min.

The morphology and size of Eu-LSiNPs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 A, Eu-LSiNPs are spherical particles, and their average particle size was approximately 2.75 nm. Circular dichroism spectroscopy indicates that the optically active chiral Eu-LSiNPs have been successfully fabricated. According to the previous reports [28], the similar CD peaks of Eu-LSiNPs and the precursor imply Eu-LiNPs inherit the chirality of l-Glu. The CD spectrum of Eu-LSiNPs shows a slight redshift compared with the precursor (Fig. 1B), which might result from the change of chirality from molecular to nanoscale caused by the variation of the chemical environment around the particles.

The FT-IR spectrum of Eu-LSiNPs was examined to ascertain its surface functional groups. It can be observed from Fig. 1C that Eu-LSiNPs possesses absorption peaks within the range of 500–4000 cm−1, namely, the characteristic absorption peaks of OH and NH situated at 3000–3650 cm−1 [29]. The absorption peak within the 2600–3000 cm−1 range is ascribed to the stretching vibration of CH [30]. The absorption peak at 1640 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of NH [31], and the absorption peak within the 1320–1390 cm−1 range represents the stretching vibration of CN [32]. The absorption peak within the range 1010–1190 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of SiOSi [33,34]. The FT-IR spectrum indicates that Eu-LSiNPs contains abundant surface functional groups. Moreover, these abundant polar functional groups also explain the excellent dispersibility of Eu-LSiNPs in water.

The elemental composition of Eu-LSiNPs was analyzed by XPS. Fig. 1D indicates that Eu-LSiNPs is composed of C, O, N, Si, S, and Eu. In the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. S1A), the peaks at 283.8, 284.8, 287.2, and 289.1 eV are attributed to CSi, CC, and CN, respectively [35,36]. The three peaks at 398.3, 399.0, and 400.1 eV in the high-resolution spectrum of N 1s (Fig. S1B) indicate the presence of NC, SiNC, and N(C)3 in Eu-LSiNPs [37,38]. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1s (Fig. S1C) reveals the existence of SiO (530.0 eV), COH (531.4 eV), and CO (532.9 eV) [39,40]. In the high-resolution spectrum of Si 2p (Fig. S1D), the peaks at 100.9, 101.5, and 102.1 eV are respectively attributed to SiC, SiN, and SiO [41]. The high-resolution spectrum of S 2p (Fig. S1E) shows two peaks at 163.2 and 167.3 eV, indicating the presence of CSC, SO2, and SO3 [42]. It can be seen from the XPS survey spectrum that there are two peaks of Eu 3d at 1134 eV and 1164 eV, corresponding to Eu 3d5/2 and Eu 3d3/2 of Eu (III) ions, respectively, and the high-resolution diagram of Eu 4d is shown in Fig. S1F [43]. These indicate the successful doping of europium. The XPS analysis results are consistent with those of FT-IR, indicating that the surface of Eu-LSiNPs has abundant functional groups.

The stability of the probe is crucial for its detection and practical applications. Therefore, the stability of Eu-LSiNPs/TC under different illumination times and temperatures was explored (Fig. S2). It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the Eu-LSiNPs/TC is nearly unchanged after ultraviolet irradiation for 60 min, indicating that Eu-LSiNPs/TC is resistant to photobleaching. When the incubation temperature increased from 30 °C to 60 °C, it could be found that its fluorescence intensity decreased slightly. In general, this Eu-LSiNPs/TC has relatively good stability. Furthermore, this ratiometric fluorescence probe has better self-calibration and accuracy than the single emission one. As shown in Fig. S3, when the NaCl concentration increased from 1 mM to 15 mM, the fluorescence intensities of Eu-LSiNPs/TC and the single emission fluorescence probe both changed. The single emission fluorescence probe changed obviously, however, the fluorescence intensity ratio of Eu-LSiNPs/TC was almost unchanged. When Eu-LSiNPs/TC and the single emission fluorescence probe was measured under the same conditions using three different instruments, it was found that their fluorescence intensities differed significantly (Fig. S4), the intensity change of the single emission fluorescence probe was obvious, but the fluorescence intensity ratio of Eu-LSiNPs/TC remained unchanged. It suggests that the good self-calibration of Eu-LSiNPs/TC can greatly improve the detection reliability and accuracy.

The chiral recognition ability of Eu-LSiNPs/TC towards different amino acids enantiomers was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 , 15 common amino acids enantiomers were tested under the same conditions. It was found that Eu-LSiNPs/TC had a significant recognition ability towards leucine enantiomers.

To achieve the optimal chiral recognition effect, the recognition conditions for leucine enantiomers, including pH, incubation time and tetracycline (TC) concentration, were optimized. As shown in Fig. S5, the best recognition effect can be obtained when the pH value is 7.0, tetracycline concentration is 30 μM, incubated for 10 min.

Under the optimal conditions, different concentrations of l-leucine or d-leucine were added to Eu-LSiNPs/TC, and the fluorescence spectra are presented in Fig. 3 . It was found that with the increase in the concentration of d-leucine, the fluorescence of Eu-LSiNPs/TC at 510 nm was enhanced, while that at 616 nm was decreased (Fig. 3A), and (F − F0)510/F616 showed a linear relationship with the concentration of d-leucine in the range of 1–28 mM (Fig. 3B), with a detection limit of 0.1 mM. While the increase of l-leucine concentration had no obvious effect on the fluorescence intensities of Eu-LSiNPs/TC at 510 nm and 616 nm (Fig. 3C), and (F − F0)510/F616 did not exhibit distinct linear relationship with its concentration, either (Fig. 3D). This indicates that Eu-LSiNPs/TC is capable of performing ratiometric recognition of leucine enantiomers.

The feasibility of Eu-LSiNPs/TC in detecting the percentage of enantiomers was determined by testing the response of Eu-LSiNPs/TC to the enantiomeric excess value (ee) of leucine enantiomers. Different percentages of leucine enantiomers were added to Eu-LSiNPs/TC, and the changes in fluorescence spectra were observed. With the increase in the proportion of d-leucine, the fluorescence intensity of the system gradually increased (Fig. 4 A), and (F − F0)510/F616 had a good linear relationship with the proportion of d-leucine (Fig. 4B), indicating that Eu-LSiNPs/TC can be used to detect the percentage of leucine enantiomers.

The possible response mechanism of Eu-LSiNPs/TC to leucine enantiomers was explored. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the FT-IR spectra of Eu-LSiNPs/TC before and after the addition of d-leucine shows no significant change, proving that no reaction occurred or bonding formed between d-leucine and Eu-LSiNPs/TC. The absorbance wavelength of Eu-LSiNPs/TC before and after the addition of L/d-leucine have almost unchanged (Fig. 5B), proving that no new conjugate system was generated. However, the absorbance intensity of Eu-LSiNPs/TC at 272 nm and 362 nm was significantly enhanced by d-leucine, but l-leucine have no effect. According to previous reports, the fluorescent of silicon nanoparticles can be enhanced by surface passivation [44,45]. We preliminary inference the enhanced fluorescence of Eu-LSiNPs/TC by d-leucine was originated from its passivation effect to the surface of silicon nanoparticles. In order to further investigate the mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of Eu-LSiNPs/TC before and after the addition of leucine enantiomers were detected. As shown in Fig. 5C, the fluorescence lifetimes of Eu-LSiNPs/TC were changed from 0.52 ns to 0.63 and 0.73 ns after the addition of L and d-Leu, respectively. Compared with l-leucine, the fluorescence lifetime of Eu-LSiNPs/TC changed more significantly by d-leucine. The fluorescence lifetime is correlated with the surface state, so this result proved the addition of d-leucine can reduce the surface defects of Eu-LSiNPs, thereby enhancing the fluorescence [46]. Zeta potentials of Eu-LSiNPs/TC before and after the addition of leucine enantiomers were also detected (Fig. 5D). It can be seen that the addition of l-leucine can make the zeta potential decrease from 12.32 to −1.71 mV, but d-leucine can make the zeta potential increase significantly. This might because d-leucine can combine stronger than l-leucine with Eu-LSiNPs/TC [47], which can reduce the surface defects of Eu-LSiNPs [48].

To further investigate the fluorescence enhancement mechanism of Eu-LSiNPs/TC at 510 nm by d-Leu, VB-XPS spectra was performed (Fig. 6 A and B). It was found that the HOMO energies of Eu-LSiNPs/TC can be enhanced by d-Leu from 1.14 to 1.64 eV [49]. The UV–Vis spectra and the Tauc Plot diagrams before and after the addition of d-Leu are presented in Fig. 6C and D. It can be calculated that the band gap of Eu-LSiNPs/TC is 3.23 eV and that after the addition of d-Leu is 3.29 eV. By using the formula ELOMO = EHOMO + Eg, the LOMO orbital energies of Eu-LSiNPs/TC before and after the addition of d-Leu was determined to be 4.37 eV and 4.93 eV, respectively. The above experimental results indicate that d-Leu can affect the molecular orbital energy (Fig. 6E), increase the band gap width, and thereby enabling the product more stable [50]. Moreover, the addition of d-Leu raises the HOMO and LOMO orbital, which can inhibition of photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [51,52]. So the fluorescence enhancement mechanism of Eu-LSiNPs/TC by d-Leu is the surface passivation and inhibition of PET.

The fluorescence spectra (Fig. S6A) and UV–Vis spectra (Fig. S6B) of Eu-LSiNPs before and after the addition of TC were examined. It was found that the fluorescence of Eu-LSiNPs redshifted and enhanced after the addition of TC, and a new peak appeared at 616 nm based on “antenna effect”, because TC could sensitize the fluorescence of Eu3+. Moreover, the absorption peaks of Eu-LSiNPs was enhanced and redshifted slightly after the addition of TC (Fig. S6B). These results suggest that there are interactions between TC and Eu3+ of Eu-LSiNPs.

To explore the roles of TC and Eu3+ in the chiral recognition process, the silicon nanoparticles without TC and Eu3+ was prepared and they showed nearly negligible response to leucine enantiomers (Fig. S7A), which is similar with that of the silicon nanoparticles with only Eu3+ (Fig. S7B). Fig. S7C illustrated the silicon nanoparticles with only TC can discriminate leucine enantiomers at some extent, and the fluorescence intensity of the entire system can be enhanced. But TC itself has weak recognition effect on leucine enantiomers (Fig. S7D). These results proved that TC plays an important role in the entire chiral recognition process. But chiral discrimination ability of the mixture of TC and Eu3+ is also very weak (Fig. S7E), so silicon nanoparticles also played some role in the chiral discrimination process.

Chiral environment usually plays a very important role in chiral recognition [53], because glutamic acid is a chiral amino acid, the role of the chiral environment constructed by glutamic acid in chiral recognition was tested. d-Glu and dl-Glu-modified silicon nanoparticles were prepared. As shown in Fig. S8A and B, their recognition ability towards leucine enantiomers was weaker than that of Eu-LSiNPs evidently. As shown in Fig. S8C, Eu-SiNPs without glutamic acid modification also showed weak chiral recognition ability. So L-Glu modified on the surface of Eu LSiNPs/TC also plays a certain role in the discrimination of leucine enantiomers.

To further explore the chiral recognition mechanism, the fluorescence spectra of valine and alanine, which have similar structures to leucine, were detected. As shown in Fig. S9, it can be found that neither d-valine nor d-alanine can enhance the fluorescence of Eu-LSiNPs/TC. Different from leucine, the carbon chains of valine and alanine are relatively short. The above mentioned results suggest the three-dimensional structure of d-glutamic acid endows Eu-LSiNPs/TC with chiral recognition capability. To sum up, the chiral discrimination ability of Eu-LSiNPs/TC was originated from the combined action of TC and l-Glu.

Chiral fluorescent silicon nanoparticles Eu-LSiNPs were synthesized in one step at room temperature. The system Eu-LSiNPs/TC formed based on Eu-LSiNPs and TC can achieve ratiometric fluorescence chiral recognition of Leu enantiomers. Its detection and recognition mechanism for leucine enantiomers is the combined result of surface passivation and inhibition of photoinduced electron transfer. The chiral environment composed of TC and l-Glu plays a significant role in the chiral recognition process. This work established a strategy for ratiometric recognition of amino acid enantiomers.
Not applicable.

Yaning Duan: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Chen Liu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Cancan Lu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xuan Liao: Validation, Software. Liwei Ma: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Cuiling Ren: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Hongli Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874061) and the Science and Technology Program of Gansu Province (No. 22JR5RA478).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chemphys.2025.112615.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Metabolism

Bioanal. Chem. 407 (2015) 7889-7898.

Anal. Chim. Acta

Spectrochim. Acta A

J. Am. Chem. Soc.
